title: ‘Ex Machina,’ ‘Annihilation,’ and ‘Men’: The Mind-bending Movies of Alex Garland
format: Sean Fennessey and Joanna Robinson walk through Alex Garland's filmography around the release of 'Men,' then Fennessey sits down with Garland on authorship, permission, and whether he still wants to direct.

films.title: Men
films.note: Garland's 10th produced screenplay and his third film as director, an A24 folk-horror freakout with Jessie Buckley opposite Rory Kinnear, who plays every man in the village. Robinson likens the one-actor conceit to a stage Turn of the Screw; both agree the birth-imagery finale is unlike anything else on screens.

films.title: Ex Machina
films.note: His directing debut and, Robinson argues, the real companion piece to 'Men' — the Gleeson and Isaac characters embody two ways men see women. Won the visual-effects Oscar and got him a screenplay nomination after ~15 years writing.

films.title: Annihilation
films.note: His biggest canvas; a box-office disappointment with loyal champions. He rewrote the VanderMeer novel heavily, treating Portman as a co-author.

films.title: The Beach
films.note: Garland's debut novel, adapted by Danny Boyle with DiCaprio; Garland has said the film has little to do with the book.

films.title: 28 Days Later
films.note: Boyle collaboration Fennessey rates among Boyle's best.

films.title: Sunshine
films.note: The other Boyle collaboration — a space movie with a garden on the ship, the nature-inside-technology motif that recurs across his work.

films.title: Never Let Me Go
films.note: His Ishiguro adaptation, unusually faithful for him.

films.title: Dredd
films.note: Written by Garland and directed by Pete Travis; star Karl Urban has said Garland drove the film's tone and execution.

films.title: Civil War
films.note: His next film, then in production with Kirsten Dunst — named as evidence he isn't waiting on 'Men's' returns before mounting the next one.

films.title: Men
films.note: Garland traces the script back ~15 years (four prior drafts), explains the Sheela na gig and green-man iconography, and defends leaving the same-actor conceit unexplained — two readings, neither right.

films.title: Come and See
films.note: His last-great-thing pick; rewatched it for his current film and found it even better than he remembered.

films.title: Possession
films.note: The Żuławski film Fennessey names as a cousin to 'Men'; Garland recommends it back to him.

films.title: The Wicker Man
films.note: The original — the folk-horror touchstone he says is hard to avoid, remake excepted.

films.title: The Evil Dead
films.note: Garland realized on the second take he'd unconsciously set up a shot straight out of it.

films.title: Stalker
films.note: Raised as a reference point back during their Annihilation conversation.

referenced.group: Also at the movies that week
